title: Big-O Analysis
subtitle: C1 — How fast your code is on data sizes you've never seen
markdown:
## Why this matters

Your code runs fine on 1,000 records. Then production data is 10 million. Now it doesn't run at all — or it crashes the database, or the page never loads. You didn't write a bug; you wrote an **algorithm whose cost grows with the input** in a shape you didn't think about.

Big-O notation is the shared vocabulary that engineers use to describe that growth shape, *before* the data gets large. It is the difference between an interview answer like "my function works" and "my function is O(n log n) — sort first, then a single pass."

If you can name the Big-O of your own code, you can predict whether it will survive scale. That's the entire reason this concept exists.

## The four families you'll see 90% of the time

1. **O(1) — constant.** Hash-map lookup. Dictionary access. Doesn't care how big the data is.
2. **O(log n) — logarithmic.** Binary search. Database B-tree index hit. Halves the problem each step.
3. **O(n) — linear.** Single loop over the input. Most reasonable code.
4. **O(n²) — quadratic.** Nested loop. Fine on 100 items; deadly on 100,000.

There are others (O(n log n) for good sorts, O(2ⁿ) for naive recursion) — but if you confidently identify these four, you're already past the bar in most code reviews.

## How to read your own code for Big-O

The rule is mechanical:
- A single pass through the input → O(n).
- A loop inside a loop, both over the input → O(n²).
- A binary search or balanced-tree lookup → O(log n).
- Constant work (no loops, no recursion) → O(1).
- Dominant term wins. O(n) + O(log n) is just **O(n)** — drop the smaller term.

When you see `array.includes(x)` inside a loop over `array`, that's O(n²) — `.includes` is a hidden linear scan. The fix is almost always: build a `Set` once (O(n)), then check membership in O(1).

## The real-world test

Before you call something "done", ask yourself two questions:

1. **What's the largest n this might see in production?** (Not today's n. Six months from now.)
2. **How does my function's runtime grow as n grows?**

If the answer to (2) is "I don't know" — you've shipped a future incident. Find out before merging.

> **Heuristic:** If your code works on 100 inputs but you've never tried 100,000, you don't know if it works.

diagrams.caption: Growth-rate intuition — same input, vastly different cost as n grows
diagrams.source:
graph LR
    N[Input size grows] --> A["O(1) Constant"]
    N --> B["O(log n) Logarithmic"]
    N --> C["O(n) Linear"]
    N --> D["O(n^2) Quadratic"]
    A --> OK[Ships fine]
    B --> OK
    C --> OK
    D --> BAD[Production incident]
    style A fill:#86efac
    style B fill:#86efac
    style C fill:#fde68a
    style D fill:#fca5a5
    style OK fill:#dcfce7
    style BAD fill:#fee2e2

tutorOpener: Let's start with a quick sanity check: can you describe one piece of code from a recent project, and tell me what its Big-O is — and how confident you are in that answer?
